verses.commentary: Jesus learned the Pharisees heard He was gaining and baptizing more disciples than John. Growing attention brings growing opposition. Jesus withdraws strategically, not fearfully.
Though Jesus Himself did not baptize, but His disciples. Jesus delegates. His presence authorizes His disciples' ministry. The work expands beyond His physical reach.
He left Judea and went back to Galilee. Strategic retreat. Timing matters. Jesus avoids premature confrontation. The hour has not yet come.
Now He had to go through Samaria. Geographical necessity or divine appointment? Jews typically avoided Samaria. Jesus chooses the direct, culturally scandalous route.
He came to Sychar near the plot of ground Jacob gave to Joseph. Historical geography. Genesis 33:19, 48:22. Jesus walks where the patriarchs walked. Continuity with Israel's story.
Jacob's well was there. Jesus, tired from the journey, sat down. It was about noon. Full humanity. Jesus experiences physical exhaustion. The incarnation is not pretend.
A Samaritan woman came to draw water. Jesus said, Will you give me a drink? Unexpected encounter. Jews do not speak to Samaritans. Men do not address women publicly. Jesus breaks cultural barriers.
His disciples had gone to buy food. Jesus alone with the woman. No witnesses to restrain scandalous behavior. Trust and mission outweigh propriety.
You are a Jew and I am a Samaritan woman. How can you ask me for a drink? She recognizes the transgression. Jews do not associate with Samaritans. Ethnic and gender barriers explicit.
If you knew the gift of God and who is asking, you would have asked Him, and He would give you living water. Jesus redirects. The real thirst is spiritual. He offers what she does not know she needs.
Sir, you have nothing to draw with and the well is deep. Literal-minded response. She sees physical impossibility, misses spiritual reality. Common pattern in John's Gospel.
Are you greater than our father Jacob? She claims Jewish patrimony through Samaritan lens. The question is ironic. Yes, Jesus is infinitely greater.
Everyone who drinks this water will be thirsty again. Physical water satisfies temporarily. Human solutions are cyclical, never final. Thirst returns.

Whoever drinks the water I give will never thirst. The water I give will become in them a spring welling up to eternal life. Internal source. Self-perpetuating satisfaction. Eternal life now, not just future.
Sir, give me this water so I won't get thirsty. She still thinks physically. Wants convenience, not transformation. Misunderstanding precedes revelation in John's pattern.
Go, call your husband. Sudden shift. Jesus exposes her life. The move seems abrupt but is strategic. Spiritual thirst connects to relational brokenness.
I have no husband. Truthful but incomplete. Jesus responds: You are right. He honors her honesty and expands it.
You have had five husbands, and the man you have now is not your husband. Divine knowledge. Jesus knows what He cannot naturally know. Her life is laid bare.
Sir, I can see that you are a prophet. She recognizes supernatural insight. The conversation shifts from water to revelation. Jesus' knowledge opens spiritual perception.

Our ancestors worshiped on this mountain, but you Jews claim Jerusalem is the place. She deflects to theological debate. Samaritan-Jewish conflict over proper worship location. Avoidance through abstraction.
Believe me, a time is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem. Geography will become irrelevant. Jesus announces the end of location-based worship.
You Samaritans worship what you do not know; we worship what we do know, for salvation is from the Jews. Jesus affirms Jewish priority in revelation history. The Messiah comes through Israel.

True worshipers will worship the Father in Spirit and truth. The hour is coming and has now come. Present and future merge. Spirit replaces place. Truth replaces ritual. Worship becomes internal, not locational.
God is spirit. Not confined to place. His worshipers must worship in Spirit and truth. Divine nature determines proper worship. Material temples give way to spiritual reality.
I know that Messiah is coming. When He comes, He will explain everything. She knows eschatological hope. Awaits future revelation. Does not recognize present fulfillment.
I, the one speaking to you, I am He. Egō eimi (ἐγώ εἰμι). Direct messianic claim. To a Samaritan woman. First explicit self-revelation in John. The marginalized hear first.

His disciples returned and were surprised to find Him talking with a woman. Cultural shock. But no one asked, What do you want? or Why are you talking with her? They restrain their questions. Trust overrides confusion.
The woman left her water jar. Forgotten mission. Transformed priority. She came for water, found living water, abandons the old pursuit.
Come, see a man who told me everything I ever did. Could this be the Messiah? Evangelism through testimony. She invites investigation, not assertion. Her question models humble witness.
They came out of the town and made their way toward Him. Her witness brings a crowd. Broken people can be powerful evangelists. Personal encounter leads to communal seeking.
Meanwhile His disciples urged Him, Rabbi, eat something. Physical concern. They think in terms of bodily needs. Jesus has been nourished in ways they do not see.
I have food to eat that you know nothing about. Spiritual sustenance. Doing the Father's will nourishes Jesus beyond physical food. Ministry feeds the minister.
Could someone have brought Him food? Literal confusion again. They miss metaphor. Physical and spiritual categories collide and confuse.
My food is to do the will of Him who sent me and to finish His work. Mission sustains Jesus. Obedience is nourishment. Completion of divine purpose is His consuming passion.
Don't you say, 'Four months until harvest'? Open your eyes and look at the fields! They are ripe for harvest. Spiritual harvest is now. The Samaritans are coming. What looks distant is immediate.
The reaper draws a wage and harvests for eternal life. Both sower and reaper rejoice together. Evangelism brings joy. Eternal fruit from present labor. Shared celebration in God's harvest.
One sows and another reaps. Proverb becomes spiritual principle. Ministry builds on prior work. Prophets sowed; apostles reap. Continuity across time.
I sent you to reap what you have not worked for. Others have done the hard work. Disciples inherit accumulated labor. They reap Old Testament sowing and John's preparation.
Many Samaritans believed because of the woman's testimony: He told me everything I ever did. Witness bears fruit. Her transformed life authenticates her words. Personal testimony leads to faith.
They urged Him to stay with them, and He stayed two days. Samaritans invite Jesus in. He accepts. Two days with outcasts. Grace crosses ethnic boundaries.
Because of His words many more became believers. Jesus' own teaching multiplies faith. Direct encounter with Jesus surpasses secondhand testimony.

We no longer believe just because of what you said; now we have heard for ourselves. We know that this man really is the Savior of the world. Firsthand faith replaces borrowed faith. Samaritans declare universal salvation. Jesus transcends Jewish-Samaritan divide.
After two days He left for Galilee. Mission accomplished. Jesus moves on. Brief stays, lasting impact. The pattern of itinerant ministry.
A prophet has no honor in his own country. Proverbial truth. Familiarity breeds contempt. Those who know Jesus' background struggle to recognize His identity.
The Galileans welcomed Him. They had seen all He did in Jerusalem at Passover. Sign-based welcome. Enthusiasm rooted in spectacle, not understanding. Fragile foundation for faith.
He came again to Cana where He turned water into wine. Return to the first sign. A royal official's son lay sick at Capernaum. Desperation drives the man to Jesus.
When he heard Jesus had arrived from Judea, he went to Him and begged Him to come heal his son, who was close to death. Parental desperation. Faith born of need. The official believes Jesus can heal.
Unless you people see signs and wonders, you will never believe. Jesus rebukes sign-dependent faith. Miracles should lead to belief, not be its prerequisite. Mature faith trusts without spectacle.
Sir, come down before my child dies. Urgent plea. The official bypasses the rebuke. Desperate need presses past critique. Parental love focuses on the child.
Go, your son will live. The man took Jesus at His word and departed. Simple command, radical trust. No visible evidence, no physical presence. Faith believes the word alone.
While still on the way, his servants met him with news: Your boy is living. Confirmation comes mid-journey. Faith is rewarded. The word proved true.
He asked when the son got better. Yesterday at one in the afternoon, the fever left him. Precise timing. Correlation between Jesus' word and the healing. Evidence confirms faith.

The father realized this was the exact time Jesus said, Your son will live. So he and his whole household believed. Faith multiplies. One healing brings an entire family to belief. Miracle leads to mature faith.

This was the second sign Jesus performed after coming from Judea to Galilee. John counts signs deliberately. Water to wine, now distance healing. Power over nature, power over illness. Glory progressively revealed.
